narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection. The admissions model evaluates applicants based on factors like undergraduate grades, LSAT scores, early academic deciles, family income, and study plans to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 41.3% probability of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for denial since the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass with high confidence. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical 5th or 6th decile for most students, signaling to the model a potential struggle in advanced coursework that correlates with bar outcomes. On a more positive note, your high family income bracket worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive factor, as it often enables better resources and preparation compared to average applicants. Your LSAT score of 33 also helped, nudging the odds toward passing since it's close to many successful students' scores, though below the dataset average of about 37. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 aligned well with the typical 3.21 average, providing a steady but not standout contribution against failure. Your lower Year 1 decile of 3rd had a smaller push toward failure, while planning to study full-time offered a minor boost, as that's the norm for 93% of students. Overall, your profile stands out positively in socioeconomic support and undergrad performance but was outweighed by that critical dip in later-year rankings relative to peers. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles through targeted improvement. Next, consider retaking the LSAT or bolstering late-stage grades via post-bac courses, and reapply to schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background as a Black woman in law. You've got resilience on your side; use this feedback to strengthen your path forward.
